name: hexagonal-advisor
description: Reviews code architecture for hexagonal patterns, checks dependency directions, and suggests improvements for ports and adapters separation. Activates when users work with services, repositories, or architectural patterns.
capabilities: Detects when domain code depends directly on infrastructure (databases, HTTP clients, frameworks)
Analyzes trait abstractions and port definitions for proper separation
Reviews domain model purity and identifies framework coupling
Evaluates adapter implementations for correct port adherence
Examines composition root patterns for proper dependency wiring
Suggests refactoring steps with complete code examples
author: EmilLindfors
securitySummary: Static analysis detected pattern matches in example Rust code that appear as false positives. All 40+ findings are confirmed false positives: sqlx::query! macros flagged as external commands, axum server binding flagged as network access, and Rust bracket patterns triggering obfuscation heuristics. This is an architecture advice skill containing educational code examples with no execution capabilities.
valueStatement: Code architecture degrades over time as teams rush to deliver features. This skill proactively identifies architectural violations in Rust codebases and suggests improvements for clean ports and adapters separation.
limitations: Does not execute code or modify files
Does not access external services or databases
Limited to pattern recognition in visible code
Cannot validate architecture without seeing the full codebase structure

useCases.title: Refactoring Legacy Rust Code
useCases.description: Senior developers modernizing Monolithic Rust services identify architectural violations and receive specific refactoring guidance with trait-based abstractions.

useCases.title: Learning Clean Architecture
useCases.description: Intermediate Rust developers learn hexagonal patterns through real code examples showing both anti-patterns and correct implementations.

useCases.title: Code Review Assistance
useCases.description: Teams establish consistent architecture by having this skill review pull requests for dependency direction and separation of concerns.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Architecture Check
promptTemplates.prompt: Review this Rust service code for hexagonal architecture compliance. Check if domain logic depends on infrastructure and suggest improvements.
promptTemplates.scenario: When you see Rust code with service or repository patterns

promptTemplates.title: Dependency Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze the dependency direction in this codebase. Are there any places where domain types depend on framework or database types? Show me how to fix them.

promptTemplates.title: Port Definition Review
promptTemplates.prompt: Review these trait definitions. Are they proper ports (domain interfaces) or do they leak infrastructure details? Rewrite them as proper hexagonal ports.

promptTemplates.title: Testing Strategy
promptTemplates.prompt: How would I test this domain logic with mock dependencies? Show me the mock implementation pattern for the repository port.

bestPractices: Define port traits in the domain layer that describe what the domain needs (driven ports) and what it exposes (driving ports)
Keep domain models pure by avoiding framework types like sqlx::FromRow or serde in domain structs
Implement all ports in the adapter layer with infrastructure-specific code, converting between domain and infrastructure types
antiPatterns: Anemic domain with structs that only hold data and no behavior - validation and business logic should live in domain types
Leaky abstractions where ports use database types like i64 or sqlx::Error instead of domain types
Skipping the composition root and scattering dependency construction throughout handlers or services

faq.question: What is hexagonal architecture?
faq.answer: Hexagonal architecture (ports and adapters) separates core business logic from infrastructure concerns. The domain defines ports (interfaces), and adapters implement those ports for databases, HTTP, or external services. Dependencies always point inward toward the domain.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Activate this skill when you see service or repository trait definitions, domain logic mixed with infrastructure code, or questions about testing architecture. It proactively analyzes and suggests improvements.

faq.question: Does this skill modify my code?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides architectural guidance and code suggestions. It only analyzes code and recommends patterns. You apply the changes yourself.

faq.question: What tools does this skill use?
faq.answer: The skill activates with Read, Grep, and Glob tools to examine your codebase. It analyzes patterns and provides textual recommendations.

faq.question: Can this skill help with testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It demonstrates how to create mock implementations of ports for unit testing, enabling fast tests without real database or HTTP dependencies.

faq.question: What languages does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill focuses on Rust and the sqlx, async-trait, and axum ecosystems commonly used with hexagonal architecture in Rust.
